coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon nanodots as efficient nitro aromatic sensor- analysis based on computational perspectives
coredata.dc:creator: Ansi, V.A
Vijisha, K.R.
Muraleedharan, K.
Renuka, N.K.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, cyan emitting carbon dots are synthesised by adopting a facile, green and economic route using table sugar as the precursor. The system is adequately characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, Zeta potential analysis, UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy. Excitation dependent emission is exhibited by these particles.The luminescence exhibited by these dots is found to be highly sensitive towards a collection of aromatic nitro compounds which enabled their sensing with admirable efficacy. Limits of detection towards the nitro aromatic systems are found to be in the range of 270–1720 Femto molar (fM), which is highly commendable while considering the benchmark values prescribed for the nitro aromaticsin aqueous systems, by the concerned authorities. The proposed carbon dot unit performed well even under complex chemical environments, as illustrated by the negligible interference of common metal salts and bio-moleculesin the sensing action. Besides, the observed sensing trend isscientifically corroborated with the aid ofwidely accepted computational analytical tools, Global reactive descriptors and Donor Acceptor Map (DAM) methods. Interpretation of sensing phenomena in a computational perspective is a novel attempt in the area of fluorimetric sensing, since works in the concerned area seldom seeks the assistance of a scientific MAP or parameter for explaining the sensing action.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Luminescence
Sensor
Nitrocompounds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dot as a Nitro Aromatic Compound (NACs) sensor
Interference of foreign analytes in NACs sensing
Computational perspective of the NACs sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Ansi V.A gratefully acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala and the research facilities received from the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut. The authors thank CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory, Pune for providing the HRTEM andCSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram for providing the AFM analysis and Surface charge analysis of the sample. Authors acknowledge CSIF (Central Sophisticated Instrumentation Facility), University of Calicut, for providing the Gaussian 09 Software support for doing computational calculations. Authors also thank Centre for Nanosciences, Amrita Institute of Medical Sciences, Kochi for providing the XPS data.

Ansi V.A gratefully acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala and the research facilities received from the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut. The authors thank CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory, Pune for providing the HRTEM andCSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram for providing the AFM analysis and Surface charge analysis of the sample. Authors acknowledge CSIF (Central Sophisticated Instrumentation Facility), University of Calicut, for providing the Gaussian 09 Software support for doing computational calculations. Authors also thank Centre for Nanosciences, Amrita Institute of Medical Sciences, Kochi for providing the XPS data.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM image (a); lattice fringes pattern (inset), XRD pattern (b) XPS profile; C1s (c) O1s (d) spectra, FTIR spectrum (e), Raman spectral profile (f),UV absorbance curve of carbon dot (g). fluorescence spectrum (h); digital image showing the cyan emission (inset), Excitation wavelength dependent luminescence emission (i); zeta potential curve of carbon dot solution (j). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The solution-state luminescence quenching profile of carbon dot solution upon the addition of different Nitro Aromatic compounds (NACs) : (a) Nitro Benzene (NB); (b) 4- Nitro aniline (4-NA); (c) 4-Nitro Toluene (4-NT); (d) 4-Nitro Phenol (4-NP); (e) 4- Nitro Benzoic acid (4-NBA); (f) 4-Nitro Benzaldehyde (4-NBAl) and (g) 4-Chloro Nitrobenzene(4-CNB).; Plot of Luminescence intensity vs concentration of concerned NACs is provided in the insets.
Limit of detection values of Nitro Aromatics.
Schematic representation of plausible mechanism involved in NACs sensing using carbon dot.
Results showing selective and interference free sensing of Nitroaromatics.
Activity order of Nitro Aromatic Compounds combining global descriptive parameters and the DAM analysis results.
Global reactive descriptors (eV) of studied compounds. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this table text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon nanodots as efficient nitro aromatic sensor- analysis based on computational perspectives

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala – 673 635, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, cyan emitting carbon dots are synthesised by adopting a facile, green and economic route using table sugar as the precursor. The system is adequately characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, Zeta potential analysis, UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy. Excitation dependent emission is exhibited by these particles.The luminescence exhibited by these dots is found to be highly sensitive towards a collection of aromatic nitro compounds which enabled their sensing with admirable efficacy. Limits of detection towards the nitro aromatic systems are found to be in the range of 270–1720 Femto molar (fM), which is highly commendable while considering the benchmark values prescribed for the nitro aromaticsin aqueous systems, by the concerned authorities. The proposed carbon dot unit performed well even under complex chemical environments, as illustrated by the negligible interference of common metal salts and bio-moleculesin the sensing action. Besides, the observed sensing trend isscientifically corroborated with the aid ofwidely accepted computational analytical tools, Global reactive descriptors and Donor Acceptor Map (DAM) methods. Interpretation of sensing phenomena in a computational perspective is a novel attempt in the area of fluorimetric sensing, since works in the concerned area seldom seeks the assistance of a scientific MAP or parameter for explaining the sensing action.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots, the eco-friendly alternative to conventional semiconductor quantum dots,are receiving enormous amount of attention in recent time owing to their non-toxicity, higher solubility, commendable fluorescence intensity, tunable fluorescence emission, remarkable photo-stability and facile synthesis routes [1–7]. Carbon nanodots are defined as quasi spherical nano particles of carbon constituting amorphousto nano-crystalline frame work, decorated with plenty of functional groups including carboxyl, carbonyl and epoxy groups. They were first identified serendipitously during the preparative electrophoretic purification of single walled carbon nano tube, which triggered immense research interests in scientific community [8]. The characteristic feature of them, vis; the luminescence can be fine-tuned by means ofsurface passivation techniques, either during the course of the reaction or after the synthesis procedure [9,10]. Intense luminescence of these particles are exploited widely in areas like chemical sensing, bio-imaging, photo-catalysis and fabrication of optoelectronic devices [11–16,1,3,5,6].

The luminescent nature of carbon dots,a matter of debate, has been a matter of curiosity to the researchers [17–19]. Surface state, core-state/size confinement and molecular fluorescence [17,18] are the most accepted suggestions in this regard. This covetable optical property has bestowed them a prominent role in fluorimetric sensors, as any species potent enough to interfere the luminescence can be sensed using the system.Accordingly, their luminescence is well exploited for tracing out a number of chemical entities ranging from simple organic molecules to complex structural frameworks including charged and neutral analytes [6,12].

Herein, the carbon dots achieved from table sugar are well characterised, and the luminescent feature of the system in presence of some common nitrogen containing organic compounds is monitored. Molecules selected for the study included both aliphatic and aromatic nitrogen bearing compounds.Amines, amides, amino acids and aromatic/ aliphatic nitro compounds are used as analytes here. Interestingly, the system responded to nitro compounds only, specifically to aromatic nitro compounds, with high selectivity, enabling identification of these species from a group of nitrogen containing organic molecules. Nitro Aromatic compounds (NACs) are involved in the manufacture of insecticides, dyes, plastics, elastomers, pharmaceuticals, fuel-additives, textiles, resins, rubber accelerators and plant growth regulators. They are associated with some acute health risks including cyanosis, and the chronic manifestation is anaemia. It may also result severe pain and redness to eye, irritation to skin and respiratory tract. According to the environmental pollution prevention concepts, traces of Nitro Aromatics (NACs) are a great threat to the safety of flora and fauna, which demand the tracing of even minute amounts of NACs in aqueous medium.

To the best of our knowledge, sensing an array of nitro aromatic compounds with the aid of a single fluorophore is nil in literature. The tested molecules include nitrobenzene and its para- substituted analogues. A general trend is observed in the affinity of these dots towards the tested NACs, which is analysed using computational methods.Previous reports on carbon dot based fluorimetric sensing discussed the selectivity, sensitivity and practical utility of the sensing tool. Further deep probing on the sensing action or systematic analysis of sensing trend is seldom seen in the literature. In this context, the present work claims the novelty of relating the results with the structure of analytes using widely accepted computational techniques like Global reactive descriptors and Donor Acceptor Map (DAM) method.

Cyan emitting carbon dots were synthesised via ammonia assisted caramelization of commercially available table sugar. For this, commercially available table sugar was purchased from the local market, subjected to caramelization at 150 °C for about 3 min in a muffle furnace. The sugar slurry obtained was then treated with 20 mL of diluted (1:1 by volume) 90 % ammonia (Merck) solution (Sugar to solution ratio 1:10). Resultant pale yellow solution was then subjected to microwave radiation assisted heating at 120 °C for 3 min. This solution was then subjected to ultra centrifugation and dialysis(against distilled water) in order to ensure the purity of carbon dots. The reaction parameters selected, vis; temperature and time of processing, were the optimized conditions which is ideal for carbon dots with fine optical features, likehigh fluorescence quantum yield, luminescence intensity etc.

Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope was utilized for HR-TEM imaging of powdered carbon dot sample. Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer with Al Ka X-ray beam was employed for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis of the sample was carried out with the aid of Rigaku MiniFlex-II diffractometer using Cu Kα1 radiation source. JASCO FTIR-4100 instrument was adopted for Fourier Transmission Infra-Red (FTIR) analysis employing KBr disc method. Raman spectrum was recorded using Lab Ram HR-Horiba Jobinyvon Spectrometer with the aid of Raman Microprobe with 532 nm Nd:YAG excitation source. Zeta potential measurement was carried out using Anton paarLitesizer™ 500 light-scattering instrument. UV–vis absorbance characteristic of the system was recorded using JASCOV-550 spectrophotometer. Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer LS 55 was employed for recording its Fluorescence spectrum.

The solution-state luminescence quenching experiment was carried out by taking a set of NACs.Nitro Benzene (NB), 4- Nitro Phenol (4-NP), 4-Nitro Aniline (4-NA), 4- Nitro Toluene (4- NT), 4- Nitro Benzoic acid (4- NBA), 4- Chloro Nitro Benzene (4-CNB) and 4- Nitro Benzaldehyde (4-NBAl) constitute this group. 5 μM stock solutions of each NACs were prepared using distilled water as solvent. Sensing studies were carried out by the addition of aliquots of appropriate concentrations of NACs in to the fluorescent carbon dot solution. Digital images displaying the fluorescence feature of the sample were captured by placing them with in LZC-4X photoreactor.

For the better understanding of electronic property and reactivities of the studied compounds, computational calculations have been employed. As density functional theory (DFT) [20] is a most commonly used computational tool especially for organic compounds,we have used the same for the present study. All the computational calculations have been carried out with DFT-B3LYP/6-31+ G (d,p) [21,22], through Gaussian 09 software package [23]. In order to get a comparison of reactivities of the compounds, Global reactive descriptors have been computed and associated donor-acceptor map (DAM) was plotted [24–26].

The utility of global descriptive parameters of a molecule gives an idea about the relation between the chemical reactivity of the molecule and its sensitiveness to structural perturbations and responses to the changes in external conditions. The global descriptive parameters include the chemical potential, electro negativity, hardness, softness, electrophilicity index, etc. These quantities correspond to the linear responses of the electron density with respect to the changes in the external potential and number of electrons [27].

Thus global descriptors are of particular relevance in comparing the properties of different molecules. The global hardness reflects the overall stability of the system [28]. The chemical hardness fundamentally signifies the reluctance towards the deformation or polarization of the electron cloud of the atoms, ions or molecules under small perturbation encountered during chemical processes. Chemical Softness is the measure of capacity of a molecule to receive electrons. More precisely it is related with the groups or atoms present in that molecule and inversely proportional to chemical hardness [29]. The chemical potential in DFT, measuring the escaping tendency of an electron from equilibrium, is accounted by the first derivative of energy with respect to the number of electrons [20], and is also the negative of electronegativity, which is a measure of the tendency to attract electrons in a chemical bond [29]. The electrophilic index tells us about the strength of electrophilicity of the species [27]. Global reactive descriptors can be calculated by two different methods, one is based on the difference in total electronic energy of neutral molecule and its corresponding anion and cation, obtained from the geometry of the neutral molecule in order to keep the external potential constant, and usually call it as 'energy vertical' [30]. The second method is by Koopmans’s theorem. However, the present work employed the energy vertical method for computing the global reactive descriptors with more accuracy. The global properties are computed by using the Eqs. (1)–(7) [31].
(1) Ionisation Potenial (IP) = Ecation – Eneutral
(2) Electron Affinity (EA) = Eneutral– Eanion
(3) Hardness (η) ≈
I P - E A
2
(4) Electronegativity (χ) ≈
I P + E A
2
(5) Softness(S) ≈
1
2 η
(6) Chemical potential (μ) ≈ -χ
(7) Electrophilicity index (ω) ≈
μ 2
2 η

The tendency of a compound to accept or donate charge is expressed by using ω+ and ω-. As ω- decreases, the compound will have a strong tendency to donate electrons, while high value of ω+ implies a strong tendency to accept electrons. Even though ionization potential and electron affinity are also related to the electron donating and accepting capacity of a compound, ω+ and ω- are more suitable for explaining the charge transfer mechanisms of the compounds. This is because of that sometimes there is only a fractional charge transfer taking place. The former parameters are significant only when there is complete transfer of electrons.
(8)
ω −
=
( 3 I E + E A )
2
/ 16 ( I E − E A )
(9)
ω +
=
( I E + 3 E A )
2
/ 16 ( I E − E A )

Donor Acceptor Map(DAM) is a plot which simultaneously shows the electron acceptance and electron donating power of the studied molecules [24–26]. Electron accepting index with respect to fluorine (Ra) and Electron donating index with respect to Sodium (Rd) are the respective parameters for DAM [32].

The formation of carbon dots from sugar is confirmed by various characterization methods. Spherical morphology with diameter around 3 nm is confirmed to the particles from the HRTEM image (Fig. 1 a). XRD pattern of the system (Fig. 1b) shows characteristic peaks at 2θ values 24° and 44.5° signifying (002) and (101) planes respectively, which is in good agreement with the literature [33]. (101) plane of graphitic origin with in plane lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is evident from its lattice fringe pattern, displayed in the inset of Fig. 1a.The XPS peak pattern of the carbon dots is displayed in Fig. 1(c and d), confirming the presence of several oxygen containing functional groups over the carbon core [34,35]. The resolved peaks of C1s XPS (Fig. 1c) profile shows three peaks at 283.5 eV, 285.6 eV and 287.3 eV which can be ascribed to the CC, CO and CO functional groups respectively. The O1s spectra (Fig. 1d) possess two different peaks at 530.8 eV and 532.4 eV corresponding to CO and COH / COC respectively. The FTIR spectral bands of the present system (Fig. 1e) substantiate the observation obtained from the XPS analysis, which showed characteristic bands of carboxyl, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups and also marks the presence of CC bonds. Raman spectral profile of the present system (Fig. 1f) further corroborates the aforesaid observations proposed by both XPS and FTIR analysis. As evident from Fig. 1(f), the system possesses two distinct bands at 1298 cm−1 and 1597 cm−1, denoting the characteristic D and G bands respectively. Intensity ratio of D and G bands, (ID/IG), was found to exceed unity, denoting the presence of abundant functional groups on carbon core, marked with excessive traps and defects. Fig. 1(g) depicts the broad UV–vis absorbance curve of the system. The carbon dot solution was found to be bright cyan in colour when irradiated with ultra-violet light (360 nm).Fluorescence spectral profile of the carbon dot system under excitation wavelength 360 nm is displayed in Fig. 1(h), which revealed a wide emission nature of the particles.Quantum yield of the solution was found to be 2.5 % (quinine sulphate is used as the standard solution).Digital image showing the cyan emission is displayed in the inset. The fluorescence emission by the system was found to be tunable, as the emission wavelength shifts gradually to red region, with increment in the excitation wavelength. The corresponding excitation dependent fluorescence emission response of the system is provided in Fig. 1(i).The intensity of these dots was found to depend on the pH of the solution. Maximum luminescence intensity was observed at lower pH values and the intensity was diminished with increase in pH. This may be due to the aggregation process, a common driving force behind luminescence reduction.At the normal pH of the as-synthesized carbon dot solution at which these experiments were made (pH = 5.5), the zeta potential was found to be -2.38 mV (Fig. 1j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dot as a Nitro Aromatic Compound (NACs) sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The intense luminescence nature of the system was utilized for tracing the presence of significant analytes. For this, a wide range of chemical entities were used to investigate whether there occurs any kind of noticeable change in the fluorescence intensity of carbon dot upon the introduction of these analytes. It was noted that among these tested nitrogen containing organic analytes, a set of aromatic nitro compounds could quench the luminescence intensity with commendable selectivity and sensitivity. The molecules responded to the test include Nitro Benzene (NB), 4- Nitro Phenol (4-NP), 4-Nitro Aniline (4-NA), 4- Nitro Toluene (4- NT), 4- Nitro Benzoic acid (4- NBA), 4- Chloro Nitro Benzene (4-CNB) and 4- Nitro Benzaldehyde (4-NBAl). No other tested analytes imparted such a palpable change in the fluorescence feature of carbon dots. Fluorescence response of carbon dot solution towards different NACs is displayed in Fig. 2 .

NACs were able to reduce the fluorescence intensity of carbon dot solution at femto molar (fM) levels of concentrations, which isappreciable whileconsidering the threshold levels prescribed by the environmental agencies. Among the NACs, 4-Nitroaniline showed extremely high sensitivity towards carbon dots. Lower limit of detection corresponding to 4-Nitroaniline was found to be 270 fM. The sensitivity of carbon dots to other molecules followed the order, 4-Nitro Toluene ≈ 4-Nitro Phenol > Nitro Benzene> 4-Nitro Benzoic acid> 4-Nitro Benzaldehyde≈ 4- ChloroNitrobenzne. The limits of detection values corresponding to 4-Nitro Toluene, 4-Nitro Phenol, Nitro Benzene, 4-Nitro Benzoic acid, 4-Nitro Benzaldehyde, 4- Chloro Nitrobenzene were found to be 370, 390,700,1200,1600 and 1720 fM respectively. Corresponding data is displayed in Fig. 3 .

Quenching in the fluorescence intensity is believed to be due to the transport of electrons from the ground or excited state of carbon dot (molecule sensor) to the ground electronic level of analyte (NACs). Electron deficient nature of nitro aromatics monopolize the possibility of strong binding interaction with the carbon dot entity enriched with excess electrons over their surface. The schematic representation of suggested electron transport between the sensor and analyte is displayed in Fig. 4 .As the exact chemical structure of carbon dot is still under investigation, one couldn’t simply suggest a single mechanistic root for explaining all the possible chemical interactions between fluorophore (carbon dot) and the aromatic analyte (NACs).

The mode of interactions between graphitic materials like graphene and aromatic molecules are suggested to be the π-π interactions between the two moieties and the dispersion interactions between the functional groups.We have recently reported the highly exfoliated graphitic (HEG) nature of carbon core of the present system [29]. In this case, the strong π-π interactions between the aromatic electron clouds of exfoliated graphitic carbon core of the fluorophore and theπ cloud of NACs is considered to be the major driving force that brings these two entities in the close proximity which in turn facilitates easy electron transfer between them. Along with the aforesaid aromatic electron cloud interactions, the existence of dispersion forces between the electron rich functionalities of both the sensor and analyte play a decisive role in this sensing strategy. Stronger the π cloud interaction, more efficient will be the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) between the sensor and the analyte. Inductive effect of the para substituent present at the parent nitro aromatic frame work determines the π electron cloud availability over the analyte surface. Nitro aromatic compound bearing electron releasing substituent at the para position possesses large π electron cloud than that of electron withdrawing para substituent bearing NACs. Increased π electron cloud at the analyte surface enhances the non-covalent interaction with the aromatic π frame work of fluorophore carbon core, which in turn results in quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots. The presence of electron releasing NH2 group at the para position makes the 4-NA as the most sensitive analyte among the selected group of molecules. The observed reactivity order of all NACs can also be substantiated on this ground, as the value of detection limit follows a particular trend in accordance with the electron releasing/withdrawing efficiency of its para substituent. The analyte with higher electron withdrawing capacity i.e., 4-CNB has the least π electron cloud availability, that leads to lesser interaction with carbon dot surface, thus leading to higher value of detection limit among the analytes. The response of NACs towards the carbon dots can be explained in a superficial way, as mentioned above. But the ambiguity related to the origin of fluorescence of carbon dots limits further authentic explanations on the observations made herein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference of foreign analytes in NACs sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of this NACs sensor under complicated chemical environment was examined by conducting the experiments in the presence of certain possible interfering analytes, such as common metal ions and bio-molecules, following the procedure given below.100 micro molar solutions of different analytes including salts of metals ions, and bio-molecules were prepared.Fe2+,Fe3+,Cd2+,Ca2+,Cu2+,Ni2+, Al3+,Zn2+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Co2+ and Ba2+constitute the metal ions selected, and glucose, folic acid, urea, ascorbic acid and methionine are included in the list of bio molecules. Surprisingly, all the tested analytes were found to exhibit negligible interference with the NACs sensing efficiency of the system, as indicated by thenil influence on luminescence. The results in this regard, expressed in terms of relative reduction in fluorescence intensity (I0-I/I0, where Io and I are the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots before and after the addition of analytes respectively) are provided in Fig. 5 . In order to prove the selectivity, the sensing action towards a representative member,4-Nitro aniline (4-NA), is also displayed in the figure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational perspective of the NACs sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized geometries of nitro aromatic compounds are displayed in Fig. 6 . These geometries were analyzed to compute the global reactive descriptors and to plot the Donor Acceptor Map. The trend observed among the Nitro aromatic compounds were systematically analyzed and subjected to detailed study with the aid of Global reactive descriptors so as to frame a scientific backup to the aforesaid NACs detection.

The global reactive descriptors are useful to compare the reactivities of a set of molecules. The electron donating/accepting capacities can be easily understood by computing the electron accepting index with respect to fluorine (Ra) and electron donating index with respect to Sodium (Rd) values and by plotting the DAM. The computed global reactive descriptors are given in Table 1 and the DAM is shown in Fig. 7 .

From Table 1, it is clear that the energy gap values are decreased by substitution at para position to the nitro group, indicating that the compounds become more reactive due to their electron releasing/withdrawing inductive effects. This is confirmed from their softness (S) values. The greater the S value, more reactive will be the compound. The electron affinity values increases for substituent having electron withdrawing inductive effects.

This is true, as they become good electron acceptors. The ‘χ’ values also support this observation. The ‘ω’ values also indicate the greater electron accepting tendency or the electrophilic nature of such compounds. In the case of compounds with electron releasing inductive effects, the electron affinity values are decreased as they are electron rich and need not accept electrons. However, their ionization energy values are significantly reduced than the parent nitrobenzene showing their electron donating capacity. Their ‘ω’ values are decreased than the parent nitrobenzene, suggesting that they are nucleophilic in nature in comparison to the other studied compounds.

The compounds can be classified into bad, good and best electron acceptor/donator by plotting DAM. Fig. 7 shows that, the compounds 1–4 act as good electron acceptors while compounds 5–7 are good electron donators. This can be explained asfollows. The compounds 1–4 carryelectron withdrawing substituents and compounds 5–7 have electron releasing substituents. So, compounds 1–4 are electron deficient which in turn make them good electron acceptors and it follows the order p-Chloronitrobenzene > p-Nitrobenzaldehyde > p-Nitrobenzoic acid > p-Nitrobenzene. The electron releasing substituents make compounds 5–7 electron rich and good electron acceptors in the order p-Nitrophenol > p-Nitrotoluene > p-Nitroaniline.

Combining the global descriptive parameters and the DAM analysis results, all the seven tested NACs can be arranged in an order of activity as shown in Fig. 8 , which is found to be in good agreement with the experimental results observed.

In summary, microwave irradiation of caramelized table sugar and ammonia mixture yielded bright cyan emitting carbon dots with fine features. The fluorescence emission of these carbon dots was exploited for the tracing of femto level concentrations of nitro aromatic compounds (NACs). The luminescence of carbon dots was quenched in presence of NACs including Nitrobenzene (NB), 4- Nitro aniline (4-NA), 4-Nitro Toluene (4-NT), 4-Nitro Phenol (4-NP), 4- Nitro Benzoic acid (4-NBA), 4-Nitro Benzaldehyde (4-NBAl) and 4-Chloro Nitrobenzene (4-CNB). The sensitivity of detection was in the order 4-NA>4-NT≈4-NP > NB> 4-NBA>4-NBAl>4-CNB. We have made a novel approach towards the sensing efficiency of carbon dots towardsNACs by analysing experimental data in computational perspective. For this, the observed quenching efficiencies of all the analytes were correlated with the global reactive descriptors and donor acceptor mapping (DAM), in order to frame the relation between the chemical reactivity of the analyte and its sensitiveness to structural perturbations and response to the changes in external conditions, which is a first attempt in fluorimetric sensing. Surprisingly, the experimental reactivity order of all NACs was in good agreement with the computational perspective, which is confirmed by their global reactive descriptors and the associated DAM plots.
V.A Ansi: Conceptualization, Investigation. K.R. Vijisha: Resources, Investigation. K. Muraleedharan: Resources. N.K. Renuka: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ansi V.A gratefully acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala and the research facilities received from the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut. The authors thank CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory, Pune for providing the HRTEM andCSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram for providing the AFM analysis and Surface charge analysis of the sample. Authors acknowledge CSIF (Central Sophisticated Instrumentation Facility), University of Calicut, for providing the Gaussian 09 Software support for doing computational calculations. Authors also thank Centre for Nanosciences, Amrita Institute of Medical Sciences, Kochi for providing the XPS data.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nano-dots induced gelation of a histidine based amphiphile: application as a fluorescent ink and modulation of gel stiffness
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Paul, K. Gayen, N. Nandi and A. Banerjee, Carbon nano-dots induced gelation of a histidine based amphiphile: Application as a fluorescent Ink and modulation of Gel stiffness,Chem. Commun. 54 (2018) 4341–4344.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excitationindependent long-wavelength emission produced by a room-temperature reaction
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. H. Yang, S. J. Zhu, Z. L. Li, Z. Li, C. Chen, L. Sun, W. Tang, R. Liu, Y. Sun and M. Yu, Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excitationindependent long-wavelength emission produced by a room-temperature reaction, Chem. Commun.52 (2016)11912–11914.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed blue-green photoluminescent carbon nanodots
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. L. Liu, B. B. Chen, Z. X. Liu and C. Z. Huang, Highly selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed blue-green photoluminescent carbon nanodots,Talanta. 161 (2016) 875–880.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence properties of graphene versus other carbon nanomaterials
Acc. Chem. Res.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of biocompatible multicolor luminescent carbon dots for bioimaging applications
Sci. Technol. Adv. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sahu, B. Behera,T. K Maiti, S. Mohapatra, Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents, Chem.Commun. 48 (2012) 8835–8837

New J. Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: V.A. Ansi is pursuing research for Ph.D. in the Department of Chemistry of University of Calicut under the corresponding author. Her research areas are Carbon nanodots and sensors.

K. R. Vijisha is presently Assistant Professor on contract in the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, India. Her areas of research interest are theoretical studies on drug designing, non-linear optics, metal sensing, conduction polymers, metal-organic frameworks, etc.

K. Muraleedharan is presently Professor in the Department of Chemistry of University of Calicut, Kerala, India. His areas of research interest are theoretical studies on drug designing, non-linear optics, metal sensing, conduction polymers, metal-organic frameworks, etc.

N.K. Renuka is presently Associate Professor in the Department of Chemistry of University of Calicut, Kerala, India. Her areas of research interests include heterogeneous catalysis, sensors, adsorption, super capacitors etc.
